_meta.note: Hand-maintained structural config. Edit this file to add chokepoints, update routes, vessel rates, or commodity mappings. Never auto-generated — safe to edit between data runs.
_meta.jsonKeyField: jsonKey maps each chokepoint to its key in simulator_data.json. null = no bilateral data yet.
_meta.terminal_straitField: terminal_strait: true marks chokepoints where the enclosed basin has no maritime exit except through this strait. Closure is an export halt, not a rerouting event. Detour costs represent supply substitution premiums, not vessel diversion costs.
_meta.ratesSource: Yahoo Finance auto-update 2026-07-08 (Brent 76.2 USD/bbl, BDI n/a)
_meta.ratesNote: Capesize charter updated to reflect BCI ~35k/d (May 2026). VLCC charter reflects mid-May TD3C TCE ~122k/d net bunker. All rates are defaults; override in simulator assumptions panel.

rateBaselines._note: Approximate market levels when vesselRates were last set (May 2026). Simulator scales charter + bunker rates proportionally from these baselines using 30d trailing averages fetched from Yahoo Finance.

hormuz.label: Strait of Hormuz
hormuz.terminal_strait_note: Hormuz is a terminal strait. All Persian Gulf export terminals (Ras Tanura, Ruwais, Basra, Qatar offshore berths) sit inside the enclosed basin. If the Strait closes, no laden vessel can exit by any alternative maritime route. Closure is a supply halt, not a rerouting event. Detour costs in this model represent the supply substitution premium: the additional shipping cost for importers sourcing non-Gulf cargo from alternative origins (e.g. Atlantic Basin crude reaching Asian buyers via Cape of Good Hope). Crude pipeline bypasses exist: Saudi East-West Pipeline to Yanbu on the Red Sea (~3 mbd, onward routing via Bab-el-Mandeb or Suez), and UAE ADCOP to Fujairah in the Gulf of Oman (~1.5 mbd, outside Hormuz). No pipeline bypass exists for LNG or LPG.
hormuz.altRoute: Terminal strait — no maritime rerouting option. Gulf cargo cannot bypass Hormuz by sea. Importers must substitute with non-Gulf supply or absorb the halt. Pipeline partial bypasses: Saudi Yanbu (Red Sea, downstream Bab-el-Mandeb/Suez exposure) and UAE Fujairah (Gulf of Oman, clean bypass for UAE crude only).
hormuz.note: Terminal strait. Rerouting cost: not applicable. Sensitivity 1.00: every percentage point of closure is fully effective. All exposed vessels are captive regardless of CII rating.

malacca.label: Strait of Malacca
malacca.altRoute: Sunda Strait (limited draft, adds 2-3d) or Lombok Strait (adds 4-6d). Capacity constrained for VLCCs.
malacca.note: ~85,000 vessel transits per year. Sunda passable for most; laden VLCCs must use Lombok.

suez.label: Suez Canal
suez.altRoute: Cape of Good Hope — adds ~7,000nm on Europe-Asia route. Typical detour 10-15 days.
suez.note: Cape diversion well-documented from 2024 Red Sea crisis. ~50 vessels/day in normal operation.

bab_el_mandeb.label: Bab-el-Mandeb
bab_el_mandeb.altRoute: Cape of Good Hope — functionally equivalent to Suez closure for most Red Sea transits.
bab_el_mandeb.note: Sequential with Suez — closure of either forces the same Cape diversion.

bosporus.label: Bosporus
bosporus.terminal_strait_note: Bosphorus is a terminal strait. All Black Sea export terminals (Novorossiysk, Constanta, Batumi, Odessa) sit inside the enclosed basin. If the strait closes, no laden vessel can exit by any alternative maritime route. Closure is a supply halt, not a rerouting event. Detour costs are zero because there is no detour available. Crude pipeline bypasses exist for some volumes: BTC (Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan) delivers Caspian crude to Ceyhan on the Mediterranean (~1.2 mbd). Russian Baltic terminals (Primorsk, Ust-Luga) bypass Bosphorus for Baltic-originated volumes.
bosporus.altRoute: Terminal strait — no maritime rerouting option. Black Sea exports are landlocked without Bosporus. Pipeline partial bypasses for crude only: BTC to Ceyhan (Mediterranean), Russian Baltic terminals.
bosporus.note: Closure means supply halt, not rerouting. Pipeline alternatives (BTC to Ceyhan, Russian Baltic ports) cover crude only.

gibraltar.label: Strait of Gibraltar
gibraltar.altRoute: No maritime bypass for Atlantic-to-Mediterranean traffic. Suez Canal (reverse routing) adds ~20 days. Limited pipeline alternatives (Maghreb-Europe) cover small volumes.
gibraltar.note: ~300 vessels transit daily. West Africa and Americas crude destined for Mediterranean ports must pass through. Atlantic-origin tankers cannot reach Mediterranean by any shorter route.

dover.label: Dover Strait
dover.altRoute: North Sea routing or NORSA — adds 1-2 days.
dover.note: Busiest shipping lane in the world by vessel count. RoRo/ferry traffic dominant.

lombok.label: Lombok Strait
lombok.altRoute: Makassar Strait or return via Malacca. Limited for very large vessels.
lombok.note: Primarily used by vessels too large for Malacca. Closure adds significant backtrack cost.

sunda.label: Sunda Strait
sunda.note: Narrower and shallower than Lombok. Draft restrictions apply to larger vessels.

luzon.label: Luzon Strait
luzon.altRoute: Alternative Philippine passages — adds 1-3 days depending on origin.
luzon.note: Key corridor between South China Sea and Pacific for East Asian trades.

red_sea.label: Red Sea Corridor
red_sea.altRoute: Cape of Good Hope — same as Suez/Bab-el-Mandeb diversion.
red_sea.note: Empirically validated against 2024 disruption. Freight rates rose 200-300% on Asia-Europe lanes.

south_china_sea.label: South China Sea
south_china_sea.altRoute: Lombok or Makassar Strait — adds 3-5 days. Viable for most vessel classes.
south_china_sea.note: Multiple alternative straits exist but all reduce transit predictability.

english_channel.label: English Channel
english_channel.altRoute: North of Scotland (NORSA route) — adds 2-4 days. Viable for most vessel types.
english_channel.note: NORSA route well-established. Primary cost is time and weather risk in northern waters.

mozambique.label: Mozambique Channel
mozambique.note: Used by Cape-route vessels and East African trade. Alternative is straightforward.

danish_straits.label: Danish Straits
danish_straits.terminal_strait_note: Danish Straits are a terminal waterway for Baltic Sea access. All Baltic export terminals are enclosed behind these straits with no alternative maritime exit. Closure is a supply halt for Baltic nations, not a rerouting event. Detour costs are zero because no maritime detour exists.
danish_straits.altRoute: No viable maritime alternative for Baltic Sea access. All Baltic trade must use these straits. Closure is a supply halt, not a rerouting event.
danish_straits.note: Gateway to the Baltic Sea. Closure is a supply halt for Baltic nations, not a rerouting event.

panama.label: Panama Locks
panama.altRoute: Cape Horn or Suez Canal (direction-dependent). US Gulf to Asia adds ~20 days via Suez.
panama.note: Panama is uniquely costly to bypass — both Cape Horn and Suez are extreme detours.

kiel.label: Kiel Canal
kiel.note: Shortcut between North Sea and Baltic. VLCCs and large Capesizes cannot transit — too wide.

st_lawrence.label: St Lawrence Seaway
st_lawrence.terminal_strait_note: St Lawrence Seaway is a terminal waterway for Great Lakes ports. All Great Lakes export terminals are enclosed with no alternative maritime exit. Closure is a supply halt, not a rerouting event. Detour costs are zero because no maritime detour exists.
st_lawrence.altRoute: No practical maritime alternative. Great Lakes ports are landlocked without this route. Closure is a supply halt for Great Lakes exporters.
st_lawrence.note: Closure is a supply halt for Great Lakes ports. Grain and steel most affected.

singapore.label: Singapore Hub
singapore.altRoute: Port Klang or Tanjung Pelepas as partial substitutes. Degradation is gradual, not binary.
singapore.note: Singapore disruption is a congestion/delay event. 0.60 sensitivity reflects partial degradation.

rotterdam.label: Rotterdam Hub
rotterdam.altRoute: Antwerp, Hamburg, or Amsterdam as partial substitutes. Degradation is gradual.
rotterdam.note: Europe's largest port by volume. Disruption is a congestion/rerouting event, not a closure.

port_said.label: Port Said / Suez Hub
port_said.altRoute: Functionally equivalent to Suez Canal closure.
port_said.note: Northern gateway to Suez. Closure triggers Cape of Good Hope diversion.

colombo.label: Colombo Hub
colombo.altRoute: Singapore or Jebel Ali as transshipment alternatives.
colombo.note: Key transshipment hub for South Asian trades. Partial substitute capacity available.

jebel_ali.label: Jebel Ali Hub
jebel_ali.altRoute: Salalah or Abu Dhabi as partial substitutes.
jebel_ali.note: Middle East's largest container port. Disruption affects Gulf re-export trades.
